sentences.text: Yeah.
I can spell it.
It's two spellings.
Because the original spelling, like it was in Polish, was N-O-W-O-G-R-O-- with an apostrophe on top-- D-E-K.
But today, it being a Russian town, or a White Russian town-- Belarus-- it's-- instead of the O with the apostrophe, they put a U.
Because they haven't got the O.
But this is in the Polish language.
In [NON-ENGLISH] in this book, she'll have correctly spelled, with the apostrophe.
But on the map it is 'Nowogrudak' with a U.
Yeah, my sister and my brother-in-law-- yeah.
Because my brother-in-law came from this town.
He has his family there.
And, well, after, you know, everything quieted down, he went to see his family, and my sister went after him.
And then I came.
And then my brother and my sister came from Bialystok, where I was staying at first.
So we all ended up in Nowogrodek.
My parents were still in Warsaw, with my two-- with my brother and my sister.
My other sister, with her husband-- still in Warsaw.
No, they couldn't come, because my sister couldn't come for a while-- another reason.
A political reason.
Because her husband was anti-Stalinist.
And he was well known in Warsaw.
And if she went-- if they went, somebody would recognize him, and now what would happen.
So she didn't go.
But she did join the Warsaw underground and worked outside the ghetto in the resistance.
Well, I was aware of the presence of Nazism from the moment it came to power.
I remember it very well.
The first elections in Germany, when Hindenburg was elected president and Hitler came to the start, you now, getting to be known, with all his marches and speeches.
Because there was no television.
We only heard it through the newspaper and through the radio.
And then, when Hindenburg died and Hitler became chancellor, we knew the whole thing.
We were very aware of it-- very politically aware family.
We knew right away that it's going to be very bad for the Jews and for the world.
But especially for the Jews.
In Poland?
Well, yes.
Poland was a very anti-Semitic country.
I didn't mix with non-Jews, as I said before.
But if you cross into this part of the street of Warsaw where there is no Jews, you couldn't be safe.
They could beat you up.
Even students-- we once went to a park and just sat on a bench, and I think a group of students came and started beating us up.
In the university, there was a ghetto in the seats.
The Jewish students had to sit on the left and the Christians and the non-Jewish on the right.
You couldn't study medicine or engineering in Poland.
You had to go overseas.
Jewish-- no secondary schools sponsored by the government.
A Jewish student couldn't enter this.
And they had to be all private.
And, generally, when the prime minister said once, at a big speech he made, he said he was against the violence against the Jews-- because there was a lot of violence.
But he doesn't mind if the Jews were-- if the Poles would took economics persecution, with boycotting and everything.
So a lot of, in the smaller towns, they had groups of Jews who didn't let Christians go into the Jewish shops, and things like this.
There was very big anti-Semitism.
And then even worse in-- you know?
Because they were right with the Nazis, as far as the Jews were concerned.
Let's go back to Nowogrodek.
What happened when you decided to stay there?
What happened?
I decided right away, because I didn't want to go back.
For me, actually, I was very happy.
I didn't-- I can't complain about the Russian-- But it was-- I knew.
I was disappointed, because I knew that, at first, nobody knew what was going on in the Soviet Union.
It was supposed to be a workers' paradise.
[LAUGH] When you see who they really are, I was disappointed.
But at this stage I was 16 years old.
And, of course, the education there was free.
One thing the Soviets did-- they appreciated education and brains.
They give you all the possibility.
If you are able, you can go as far as you could go as far as education is concerned.
So I didn't have where to go.
My sister lived at the other side on her husband's farm.
And they were also-- they also came from Warsaw.
They had nothing.
My sister had to leave all her possessions in Warsaw, because we were walking.
So they were pretty, you know, not-- So somebody said, go into an orphanage.
I said, I'm too old for an orphanage.
They said, just get in.
Even if you are one month there, they'll take you under their protection.
You're protected, you know, and everything.
They'll look after you.
So somehow, with some strings being pulled, I went into the orphanage.
I stayed there for a few months.
Do you remember the name?
Uh, it really-- it was just called the Kinderheim-- the house for children.
Of course-- and I went right and enrolled into school.
And I had to sit for exam.
And, of course, I passed it.
And I skipped a few classes, as I was still so much ahead of everybody else.
Because the small towns, they weren't too much exposed to culture or to-- you know?
Even the good students, they were small-town-ish.
You know how it was.
So I was so much ahead and everything else.
But after a few months, they opened a college, a teachers' college.
And the people in the children's home-- they made with us-- there were three girls.
They made with us a pact that we can stay a little longer if we helped with the younger children.
So we were sort of half looking-- half looking after the younger children.
And we had our food and our shelter there.
But then came that after 14 you can't stay.
So they opened a teachers' college which had a dormitory and everything-- board-- like a boarding school.
So we went to there, and I sat for exam.
I sat--
It was also a couple of years ahead of me.
And I sat for exam, and I passed.
And they took me into the college, and I lived there, you know?
So, for most of the time, I was studying to be a teacher.
Were you in regular contact with your sister and brother?
Yeah.
With my sister and brother.
And my brother-- yes, this was a different story.
My brother, when he went to try to get my parents to the border, he got caught and didn't have any papers.
He was sent to Siberia.
So he wasn't-- when the Nazis reoccupied Nowogrodek, he wasn't with us.
He was in Siberia.
My sister was sending him parcels.
And my other sister got work.
She start working in Nowogrodek, and I went to school.
So we were all well set up.
And then came, uh-- We didn't get real passports.
We were registered as refugees.
Not my sister, because my sister was the wife-- my older sister-- she was the wife of a citizen.
Because he was born there and he lived there.
So she became automatically a citizen.
And I was a refugee.
And there, the KGB was calling me every three months, checking on me.
And at one stage, they told me to leave Nowogrodek, because it's too close to the borders-- to the other borders-- to the Lithuanian border, at this particular time.
And I said, where will I go?
That's where I have got my sister, I've got my school.
And they said, oh, the Soviet Union is big.
You'll find a spot-- somewhere, by myself.
And they didn't want to give me a passport.
But after a while the headmaster from my college interfered.
He said we're under his protection, and they are only children and they are only students with me and somebody else.
And they gave us an extension for another three months-- passport or document.
And then they annexed Lithuania.
In 1940, I think.
They annexed-- Lithuania has become a Soviet republic-- all the three Baltic states.
So it became irrelevant, because I was no longer close to the border.
You had to be, I think, 140 kilometers from the border.
So this became irrelevant, so I stayed.
And I actually enjoyed my stay there under the Soviet occupation.
Because I was doing what I wanted to do.
I was studying.
I was happy, I had-- I wasn't persecuted.
And I was happy.
Really, I only miss-- I had terrible homesick.
And I had this experience as a-- from the month of bombing and starving and everything.
So my roommates were telling me that, when I went to bed-- they would study much longer than I did.
I didn't like studying too long.

When they studied and I went to sleep-- we all slept and studied in the same room-- they told me that I had a nightmare-- that I was screaming out from my sleep, you know, this-- And I was terrible homesick.
I was missing my parents.
I was missing my town.
I was missing my brothers and sisters and missing my friends.
Yeah.
Up till '41, until the Soviets were attacked by the Germans, in 1941.
Which time?
Yeah.
All of a sudden, I remember-- it was the end of the year.
We'd just finished our exams.
Getting ready to celebrate the end of the year, and everybody contributed towards the celebration.
And, all of a sudden, we were sitting in our room, and we heard airplanes.
And we thought, oh, well, somebody's passing.
The last thing on my mind was that the Soviet Union is being attacked.
And we didn't take any notice of it.
And then we heard a whole lot of airplanes-- and bombs.
And the headmaster called us downstairs into the assembly hall and said, no, the big-- the Nazis-- before, he said, the capitalists-- because they were allies with the Germans, in 1939 to '41.
So they're always talking about the Americans, about the English or whatever, as their enemies.
And all of a sudden, the Germans have become their enemies.
And they attacked, and we've got to fight-- we will have to fight, and we'll drive them out.
And he was so sure of it.
And so was I, you know.
They had such big propaganda in the Soviet Union about how mighty they were, and they were showing it on the streets-- the marching and the tanks and everything.
And, for a moment, I was happy-- that they attacked there.
I said, now the Soviet Union will push-- throw out the Germans from Warsaw.
We'll be back to family.
We will reunite.
That was my first reaction, because I was so positive that the-- how could Germans, who were at war with so many countries, attack such a might as the Soviet Union and not be pushed back?
And, sadly, that wasn't the case.
In about three days, they engaged, and they took us-- they assembled us and the things.
They said everybody's going to contribute to the war effort.
First we went to a big hall in the city, and we sat all night, writing conscription for the enlistment-- letters for the all-- people who were-- uh-- for-- how do you call it?
Who need to go to the war.
Conscription.
Yeah.
Conscription letters-- conscript-- so we sat all night and wrote all those conscriptions letter--
Huh?
The whole school, almost everybody, you see, you know.
We've got to start from somewhere.
So they said, we'll go to this hall, and we'll take the list of all the people at this particular age group, and we'll pick them out from the list of citizens and send them letters.
So we wrote those letters and filled those out.
And then we were trying to-- in our college, it was the end of the year, so we're trying to convert it to a hospital.
So we helped there.
And then, all of a sudden, they were running.
The Russian army was running.
They came from the borders, and they said everybody's in a panic.
Everybody's running away.
Such a mighty blitzkrieg that they haven't got a chance.
They said it quietly.
And the Russian army was trapped, because they attacked them from one side and they were not prepared at all.
Oh, completely.
I was absolutely devastated to see the Germans back again.
It was absolutely-- I remember when they marched in, my heart sank.
And-- said, what's the use?
And here it comes.
And one couple, I remember, went up to the attic and hanged themselves.
They didn't want to see the Germans again.
And it was very, very sad, you know?
To see them-- oh.
My father used to say, the Germans, they will conquer the world.
He saw that war machine that walked in into Warsaw and everything.
What they did to the Polish army in one day-- in two days, in three days.
Only Warsaw put out a resistance.
Nobody put out a resistance.
They just walked in everywhere.
And after they engaged with England, and with France, and with the whole-- almost all Europe-- and they were in Africa, and they were everywhere.
And then the Germans came in.
They said, in six weeks-- in six days, I think-- we'll be in Moscow.
Yes, of course I remember.
It was the 22nd of June, 1941 when they attacked us.
And my brother-in-law was then on the border.
He was a locksmith.
And they were building a new airport.
So he was called up for six weeks, to help in the building of the airport-- like conscripted, almost.
Not willingly.
He was conscripted to do it.
And he was the first casualty.
Because it was right on the border, and they attacked-- first thing, they attacked the airport which was being built.
And the night before, they were told that all the-- somebody who escaped told us that all the airplanes have to be taken apart, because there was a fault in them.
And so everything-- not even one airplane was ready to take off.
No, no, I went right to my sister's place.
You know?
Me and my other sister went right to my sister's place.
They were bombing Nowogrodek, too.
And half the town escaped, because it was a bit-- not in the center of the town-- there's a little farming community right beside the thing in Nowogrodek.
And everybody escaped, because they were safer there, because there were no big buildings or anything, so they escaped.
So this farmhouse was full with-- you couldn't put things in there.
So they were staying for ages there before they could go back to establish some sort of life.
Everything was overcrowded.
It wasn't-- I would have, but it was not possible.
Because they put in-- the Germans were fighting like-- they started from here, and then, with airplanes, they put a whole army down by parachutes.
And they blocked the way.
The Red Army was trapped.
I don't know how many hundreds of thousands.
With their tanks, with their arms, with everything.
They were trapped.
And the roads leaving out of-- it was blocked to Minsk-- that was the only way we could have escaped-- was blocked.
And lots and lots of refugees.
And the airplanes will go down-- they mow them down with the machine guns.
There was no way of escaping.
Yeah.
No, not at first, no.
At first, when the Germans came in, the first thing they did, they said, we have to organize the Jewish community.
So let's go and make a meeting.
All the prominent people, like lawyers, doctors, state counselors, all sort of prominent people.
We'll have a meeting in the city hall, and we'll see how to organize the Jews.
And the ones that were vain went there.
The ones that had a bit of more-- [LAUGH] they could reason better, you know, didn't go.
Nobody has seen them since.
First thing-- that was their policy.
They liquidated the leaders of the community.
That was right after they entered-- the same week, that.
We need to organize the Jews, and we need all the prominent citizens to come in town, so we can talk things over and organize the community.
So they went, and nobody has seen them.
They were the first to go.
And then they had some, uh, casual shootings.
They'd just go to the marketplace, with a lot of shops, and they'll pick up 50 Jew people and just put them-- line them up in the market and just shoot them.
For no reason whatsoever.
I remember they had 49, and there was one still open-- a pharmacy.
They went into the pharmacy, and they took out the pharmacist Jew.
Because they needed 50.
We were witnessing it.
I wasn't there, but a lot of people were there.
And then they were all dead, and they went to put-- the cemetery.
We knew the people.
It was a town that everybody always knew everybody else.
And we saw it.
I didn't see the-- when it happened, but other people did.
And then they were just casually catching up people.
They used to call, you know, let's go-- we need some workers somewhere, to do a job.
And they load up a truck full of people, and nobody saw them again.
They disappeared.
That's what they did at first.
There was no ghetto yet.
And everybody had to, of course, put right away an armband, with a thing.
That was first.
And then came the Stars of David and the yellow ones on the front and on the back.
But first were the things, the arm-- I think they-- I don't know which came first.
Just a Star of David, and that was enough to show that you-- just enough.
And they told everybody, now every Jewish house has to have a big placard on it, on front, and to write down, this is a Jewish house.
So the Germans-- they were free to go and take what they want.
And we're not protected from anything.
Anything could be done to people of this house.
So we lived-- and every day, we had to go to the-- then they established a Judenrat, which was a Jewish council.
And it was in the middle of the town, in one building.
And everybody who was able-bodied had to come every day and register for work.
And, from there, they sent groups everywhere.
So I used to go to work.
At first, I worked in clearing the snow, chopping the ice, clearing the bricks from the bomb sites, going to clean houses prepared for the German-- the officers, or for the German families.
On farms together, the-- all sort of things.
Pick potatoes.
The snow was the worst-- clearing the snow.
[LAUGH] Because usually we used to go-- it was-- Nowogrodek is a very cold town.
It's the coldest spot, I think, in whole Poland it was.
Because it was on a hill, and it stood unprotected.
And the cold-- the cold in Poland severe-- I remember going to school in 30 degrees minus, Celsius.
And after that, after 30, we could stay home and not go to school.
And the snowstorms were absolutely-- these icy winds, where the snow beats you.
It was-- and, being a hilly place, it became slippery.
You had to go with a bag of ash and put, you know.
Or with something, or chop something-- chop up, so you can-- just like you see the people climbing mountains.
Slippery and so hilly.
Up and down, up and down, like this.
Oh, we work from, you know-- till dark.
Being winter, it was earlier than in summer.
Just work.
Never got paid or any food, any-- nothing for this.
This was just-- and everybody wanted to go to work, because in this way they thought they'll be safe, you know?
The ghetto was established like this.
There were originally about 6,000 Jews.
I think some say 7,000, some say 5,500.
Roughly 6,000 Jews.
And one day I'm coming down from-- coming home from work, and I see this big placard which says, in German, that the German-- the Third Reich is involved in war.
And we need-- they need every help in their war effort.
So they have to be very efficient and see how they can utilize, in the best way, everybody's contribution.
So the Jews who are tradesmen, professional-- the young ones, the able ones, with their families-- it will benefit the German Reich if they live together, so they can be more efficiently utilized.
So the first thing-- that's what I read.
On Friday, I remember, it was.
That's what I read.
And then the other question-- what become of the not able and not, uh-- the ones not tradesmen or craftsman people who are going to be utilized-- not utilized for the German effort?
This was clear, what's going to become of them.
So this was a week before.
And, at the same time, they gave an order that all Jews must give up all their possessions-- valuables.
So, like, gold watches, coins, art objects-- anything that's valuable.
And failing to do this is punished by death sentence.
So you should have seen the-- you should have seen the queue that queued up, in this frosty-- this November-- end of November-- this frosty day.
And everybody was giving up their valuables.
They thought, you know, this way they're going to buy a life.
Because the others that don't give will be punished by death.
They'll assume that they're not giving.
I didn't have anything to give.
So the people with whom we lived said, oh, we have a few things.
We'll give you a watch.
We'll give you a ring.
We'll give you this.
So we're going to give it away altogether, so they says, we'll divide it.
I said, no, I'm not giving away anything, because that's like bandits or-- they'll take your money, and they'll take your life.
[LAUGH] That's how they did it.
Some people didn't understand that.
They said-- they actually were giving up piles.
You should have seen what they collected there.
And so, you know-- And the week after came that placard.
So they already had the possessions, and now they can-- it was all according to plan.
So--
Uh-- which possessions?
Possessions.
Yeah, everything.
Rings and watches and chains and gold-- silver objects, gold, paintings, everything.
There was-- it was quite a wealthy little town.
And they thought they were buying their life.
And, of course, a week later came this placard.
And that one-- well, the ones-- they will establish a ghetto for those able, useful, fit, young, skilled people, and they didn't say anything about the rest.
For them there would be the ghetto.
So the German Reich will now have to work more efficiently, you know?
With everybody being together, all the contributors being together.
So a week later, there was an order for the whole community.
Is it stopped?
Yeah.
Already another half hour?
